coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent protein nanoparticles: Synthesis and recognition of cellular oxidation damage
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Bing-Qiong
Jin, Jian-Cheng
Zou, Han-Feng
Wang, Bei-Bei
He, Huan
Jiang, Peng
Jiang, Feng-Lei
Liu, Yi
coredata.dc:description: Intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation are associated with many diseases. Lots of studies focus on the detection of intracellular ROS by small fluorescent molecules. However, ROS recognized by biocompatible nanoparticles are relatively less reported. It is widely known that albumin-based nanomaterials possess unique advantages in biomedical applications because they are biodegradable and biocompatible. Herein, fluorescent protein nanoparticles (PNPs) were prepared using BSA as a starting material without introducing extra fluorescent molecules. The blue fluorescent PNPs were well characterized by FL, FTIR, CD, TEM, DLS, etc. It was revealed that the PNPs exhibited two types of emissive centers through FL spectra and the fluorescence lifetimes. Further mechanism study indicated that the fluorescence of the PNPs was mainly derived from three kinds of aromatic amino acids, namely tryptophan, tyrosine and phenylalanine. Moreover, the fluorescence properties of the PNPs were tightly related to pH. The PNPs displayed excellent stabilities under harsh conditions as well as physiological conditions. In addition, the PNPs (200 μg/mL) were nontoxic to HeLa and GES-1 cell lines, showing good biocompatibility. The cellular uptake of PNPs was occurred only when the cells were stressed with glucose oxidase or H2O2, thereafter the bright blue fluorescence was observed, indicating that it could be utilized for the recognition of cellular oxidation damage. These findings will offer novel clues for the future synthesis of even brighter protein nanoparticles and their biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Protein nanoparticles
BSA
Fluorescence
Oxidation damage
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of the synthesis of PNPs
Mechanism study on the origin of PL

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Effects of the concentration of NaOH, (b) the reaction temperature, (c) the mass of BSA, (d) the reaction time on the syntheis of PNPs.
(a) TEM images of PNPs and the inset was the size distribution of PNPs. (b) The hydrodynamic diameter of native BSA and PNPs. The polydispersity index of PNPs was about 0.452.

(a) 1- absorption spectra of native BSA; 2- absorption spectra of PNPs. 3- excitation spectra of PNPs; 4- emission spectra of PNPs. The insets were photographs of PNPs in daylight (left) and under 365 nm UV light (right). (b) PL properties of the PNPs with different excitation wavelength. (c) FTIR spectra of native BSA and PNPs. (d) CD spectra of native BSA and PNPs.

(a) PL spectra of the crude product for PNPs synthesis. (b) PL spectra of the product using tryptophan, tyrosine, phenylalanine as precursors, the mass of three kinds of acids were equal to which of the BSA contained in the process of preparation of PNPs. (c) PL spectra of the product using tyrosine and phenylalanine as precursors, the mass of tyrosine and phenylalanine were equal to which of the BSA contained in the process of preparation of PNPs. (d) PL spectra of the product using tryptophan as precursors, the mass of tryptophan was equal to 10 times which of the BSA contained in the process of preparation of PNPs.
PL Intensity of the PNPs under different pH value. Error bars represent standard error of the mean, n = 3.
PL spectra of the PNPs under different pH value.
Absorption spectra of the PNPs under different pH value.

Confocal photographs of HeLa cells incubated with 200 μg mL−1 PNPs in the absence and presence of 0.2 ng/mL GO, the excitation wavelength was 405 nm. (a) The bright-field image of Hela cells incubated with PNPs. (b) The fluorescence image of Hela cells incubated with PNPs. (c) The fluorescence image of HeLa cells incubated with PNPs in the presence of GO. (d) Merged image of HeLa cells incubated with PNPs in the presence of GO. The scale bar was 20 μm.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent protein nanoparticles: Synthesis and recognition of cellular oxidation damage

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

College of Chemistry and Material Sciences

Wuhan University of Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Virology & Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (MOE), College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Virology & Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (MOE)
College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation are associated with many diseases. Lots of studies focus on the detection of intracellular ROS by small fluorescent molecules. However, ROS recognized by biocompatible nanoparticles are relatively less reported. It is widely known that albumin-based nanomaterials possess unique advantages in biomedical applications because they are biodegradable and biocompatible. Herein, fluorescent protein nanoparticles (PNPs) were prepared using BSA as a starting material without introducing extra fluorescent molecules. The blue fluorescent PNPs were well characterized by FL, FTIR, CD, TEM, DLS, etc. It was revealed that the PNPs exhibited two types of emissive centers through FL spectra and the fluorescence lifetimes. Further mechanism study indicated that the fluorescence of the PNPs was mainly derived from three kinds of aromatic amino acids, namely tryptophan, tyrosine and phenylalanine. Moreover, the fluorescence properties of the PNPs were tightly related to pH. The PNPs displayed excellent stabilities under harsh conditions as well as physiological conditions. In addition, the PNPs (200 μg/mL) were nontoxic to HeLa and GES-1 cell lines, showing good biocompatibility. The cellular uptake of PNPs was occurred only when the cells were stressed with glucose oxidase or H2O2, thereafter the bright blue fluorescence was observed, indicating that it could be utilized for the recognition of cellular oxidation damage. These findings will offer novel clues for the future synthesis of even brighter protein nanoparticles and their biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the inception of X-ray technology for medical imaging, many non-invasive methods and technologies such as computed tomography (CT) [1], magnetic resonance (MR) [2], positron emission tomography (PET) [3], single photon emission CT (SPECT) [4], ultrasound [5] and optical [6] imaging have been introduced to clinical diagnosis. Among them, optical imaging has currently become a hot topic and attracted increasing attention because of the rapid development of fluorescent materials and fluorescent imaging technology. As star materials, quantum dots (QDs) which possess superior fluorescence emission, broad excitation and high photostability properties, have been widely used in bioimaging especially in early diagnosis of disease [7]. However, most high-performance QDs contain toxic elements such as cadmium (Cd) and lead (Pb), which limit their practical applications. In addition, they could not reveal the inner reason during the determination of diseases when specific detection groups are not functionalized. Therefore, fluorescent nanomaterials with excellent biocompatibility and specific detection function are of urgent need.

Albumin is the most abundant plasma protein with a molecular weight of 66.5 kDa. Human serum albumin (HSA) exhibits many vital functions and properties, such as solubilizing long chain fatty acids, binding various of therapeutic drugs, binding Copper (II), Nickel (II), Calcium (II), Zinc (II) and acting as the ion transport vehicle in the blood and keeping the balance of the colloid osmotic pressure of the blood [8]. Materials based on cellular albumin proteins possess unique advantages during the practical use in organisms. In the past decades, albumin and its nanoparticles have emerged as versatile protein carrier for drug targeting and for improving the pharmacokinetic profile of peptide or protein-based drugs due to their biodegradable and nontoxic properties [9–13]. Typically, protein nanoparticles (PNPs) are prepared through emulsion/solvents evaporation, coacervation or desolvation and polyelectrolyte complexation methods. The sizes of the most reported PNPs prepared by these processes are between 100 and 1000 nm [14]. There are also a few reported processes such as assembling by free sulfhydryl groups [15], control the aggregation of proteins and then cross-link the proteins [16], which can obtain PNPs of sub-100 nm. It would be of great interest to develop highly emitted fluorescent PNPs with specific function.

Reactive oxygen species (ROS) and oxidative stress play essential roles in many important biological processes especially in inflammatory response and apoptosis, which have been considered as a major factor for nano-toxicity in the preclinical test. ROS also affects many signal routes such as p53 and JNK signal pathways [17]. In addition, intracellular ROS generation are associated with many diseases such as carcinogenesis, neurodegeneration [18], atherosclerosis [19], diabetes [20], and aging [21]. The increase of ROS would lead to oxidative stress, which could damage structural and functional macromolecules such as nucleic acid [22], protein [23], and lipid [24]. Usually, the harmful effects of ROS may be manifested by DNA damage, oxidation of poly-unsaturated fatty acids in lipids and oxidation of amino acids in proteins. There are many studies focused on the detection of intracellular ROS [25–28], most of which are small fluorescent molecules. The biocompatible nanoparticles have been relatively less reported. Herein, blue fluorescent PNPs were prepared via a simple, one-pot synthetic route and fully characterized. Besides, the fluorescence mechanisms and stabilities of the PNPs were furthered explored. The results indicated that two types of emissive centers in PNPs were derived from three kinds of aromatic amino acids (tryptophan, tyrosine and phenylalanine). Besides, the fluorescence properties of the PNPs were closely associated with the pH values and the PNPs remained stable under several poor conditions and physiological condition. Moreover, the PNPs were nontoxic to HeLa and GES-1 cell lines, showing excellent biocompatibilities and they could be used to identify the cellular oxidation damage through cell imaging.

All reagents were employed from commercial sources without further purification, if not stated otherwise. Glucose oxidase (GO) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. BSA, NaOH, hydrochloric acid, tryptophan, tyrosine, phenylalanine, hydrogen peroxide, 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) etc. were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water with 18.2 MΩ·cm (Millipore) was used in all synthesis. The commercial nanoparticle (CNP) was purchased from Wuhan Jiayuan Quantum Dot Company.

Typically, a mixture of 0.4 g of BSA and 25 mL of NaOH (2 mol/L) was vigorously stirred at 80 °C for 60 h. Then the pH of the mixture was adjusted to 5.0 through the addition of 2 mol/L of HCl (aq) and vast precipitates were observed. The mixture was centrifuged with a speed of 6000 g for 10 min. Finally, the precipitates were discarded and the products were freeze-dried and obtained after the supernatant liquid was dialyzed in deionized water using a 3500 Da membrane for 12 h.

UV-vis absorption spectra were measured with a UV-61 OOPC double-beam spectrophotometer (MAPADA, China). Fluorescence spectra were carried on a LS-55 spectrofluorometer (Perkin Elmer, USA). The fluorescence decay curve of the PNPs was measured on a FLS920 system (UK, Edinburgh). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded by Thermo (USA) FTIR spectrophotometer and KBr crystals were used as the matrix for sample preparation. Far-UV circular dichroism (CD) spectra were recorded on an Applied Photophysics (UK) automatic recording spectrophotometer at 25 °C by a quartz cuvette with a 0.1 cm path length and PBS buffer was used as a control. The morphology of the PNPs was observed by a JEOL 2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEOL, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The hydrodynamic diameter was measured by Nano-ZS ZEN3600 (Malvern Instruments, Malvern, UK).

Quinine sulfate (quantum yield = 0.54) was chosen as the standard. The quantum yield of PNPs was determined according to the following equation:
QY ( sample ) = ( F sample/ F ref ) ( A ref/ A sample ) (
n 2
sample/
n 2
ref ) QY ( ref )
QY is the quantum yield. F is the area of the fluorescence emission spectrum. A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength and n is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript sample and ref denote sample and standard respectively. The excitation wavelength was 340 nm.

The Human cervical cancer (HeLa) and Human gastric epithelial (GES-1) cell lines were obtained from the China Center for Type Culture Collection (CCTCC, Wuhan, China). HeLa cells were cultured in dulbecco’s modified eagle medium (DMEM) supplied with 10% fetal bovine serum, 1% penicillin, and 1% amphotericin at 37 °C. The cells were seeded into 96-well plate and allowed to adhere for 24 h. After incubation with different concentrations of PNPs for 48 h, MTT with a concentration of 0.5 mg·mL−1 was added into each well. The cells were allowed to grow for another 4 h until purple precipitate were detected. Then the medium was removed and 150 μL dimethyl sulfoxide was added. The clusters were vibrated for 10 min to dissolve the crystals completely. Finally, the absorption at 490 nm was measured by Elx800 Micropalte reader (BioTek, USA).

HeLa cells were seeded in culture dishes and cultured at 37 °C. The PNPs were diluted with fresh cell culture medium to the specific concentrations (200 μg/mL). After incubation for 4 h, the cells were washed with phosphate buffered saline (PBS) three times and used for confocal fluorescence imaging. The extent of staining was visualized by Nikon A1 Confocal laser scanning microscope equipped with a spectral detection system. The concentration of added GO and H2O2 were 0.2 ng/mL and 1 mmol/L, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the synthesis of PNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of NaOH, the reaction temperature, the mass of BSA and the reaction time were investigated to understand the conditions that influence the synthesis of PNPs. Firstly, we optimized the concentration of NaOH. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the PL intensity of the PNPs first gradually increased as the concentration of NaOH went up (0.2–2.0 mol L−1). However, when the concentration of NaOH reached 3 mol.L−1, the fluorescence intensity went down. As the concentration of NaOH further increased, the PL intensity of the PNPs further decreased, which was probably due to the poor dispersity of BSA in high concentration NaOH solution and the quenching effect. Besides, the fluorescence peak showed a blue shift. It was probably due to the elevation of the alkalinity, which may influence the electron distribution of the emissive centers of PNPs. The optimized reaction temperature was 80 °C (Fig. 1b). The reaction temperature mainly influenced the aggregation of the proteins. As could be seen from Fig. 1c, the PL intensity of the PNPs first increased as the mass of BSA increased (0.1–0.4 g), then declined as the mass of BSA continued increasing (0.4–0.8 g). The fluorescence peak displayed a little red shift with the enhancement of the mass of BSA, which was probably a result of concentration effect. The time course measurements of the fluorescence evolution of the PNPs were displayed in Fig. 1d. During the reaction process, the color of the mixture gradually changed from colorless to light yellow, and then to yellow green. After reaction for about 60 h, PNPs with the best PL properties was obtained. The time course PL intensity of the crude PNPs at 482 nm was displayed in Fig. S1 and it was well fitted with an exponential decay function, indicating the reaction might be a pseudo first order reaction. Based on the above data, we concluded that the PL properties of the PNPs are mainly related to the synergistic effects between the solubility of BSA and the concentration of NaOH. The optimized reaction condition was that BSA (0.4 g) and 2 mol L−1 of NaOH (25 mL) were reacted at 80 °C for 60 h.

The morphology and size distribution of the PNPs were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the PNPs were monodisperse with a narrow size distribution (24–36 nm). The statistical diameter of the PNPs was 29.88 ± 0.14 nm. The size distribution of the PNPs agreed well with the Gaussian distribution and the full width half maximum (FWHM) of the fitted curve was 2.0 nm, which further confirmed the narrow size distribution of the PNPs. The hydrodynamic diameter of the PNPs was detected by Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS). As displayed in Fig. 2b, compared with the native BSA, which had a hydrodynamic size of 8.4 nm, the PNPs had a much larger hydrodynamic size of 80.5 nm, indicating the formation of PNPs. It was worth noting that the hydrodynamic size of the PNPs was much larger than that measured by TEM. It was due to the different processes that involved in the preparation of the sample. In the case of the TEM method, images depicted the actual size at the dry state of the nanoparticles, whereas the size measured by the light scattering method was a hydrodynamic diameter (hydrated state). Therefore, the PNPs displayed a much larger hydrodynamic volume due to the solvent effect in the hydrated state [29]. In addition, proteins usually have flexible structures. Thus, the difference in the size of the PNPs measured by TEM and DLS could become more obvious.

Some explanation is essential about the name of our prepared nanoparticles since it is not common. Several groups fabricated carbon dots by using BSA as carbon sources. Ng and his colleagues used BSA as starting materials to prepare carbon dots using concentrated sulfuric acid as dehydrating and carburizing agent at 50 °C [30]. Researchers also heated BSA solution at about 200 °C by a hydrothermal method to obtain carbon dots [31–33]. While the formation of the carbon dots underwent the process of carbonization, which was the intrinsic process of the formation of carbon dots by using bottom-up method. In our work, neither strong dehydrating nor carburizing agents such as concentrated sulfuric acid and phosphoric acid were added. Besides, high temperature and high pressure were also not involved. Therefore, the carbonization process could not happen and carbon dots were not formed. In addition, the sizes of the carbon dots were generally less than 10 nm and the morphologies of the carbon dots were obviously different from our PNPs. In our results, the boundary of the PNPs was not much clear, which also indicated that the nanoparticles were not carbon dots. Based on these facts, we could conclude that our nanoparticles were different from carbon dots. Thus, we religiously named the nanoparticles fluorescent protein nanoparticles (PNPs). And we measured the sizes of the PNPs by negative staining technology.

Then we examined the optical properties of the PNPs. As displayed in Fig. 3 a, the PNPs exhibited two absorption peaks at 223 nm and 275 nm, which could be attributed to the absorption of amide bond and aromatic amino acid residue. The fluorescent PNPs showed excitation and emission peaks at 330 and 426 nm, respectively. The pale yellow PNPs solution emitted an intense blue fluorescence under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light (inset of Fig. 3a) and the PL quantum yield (QY) was about 2.7% (Fig. S2), which was a little lower than the CNP (7.8%). The PL spectra of the CNP was displayed in Fig. S13. Interestingly, the PNPs showed excitation independent PL properties excited from 280 to 320 nm. However, when the excitation wavelength changed from 330 to 420 nm, they presented excitation dependent PL properties (Fig. 3b). These results indicated that the PNPs contained two types of emissive centers. To verify this speculation, the fluorescence lifetime of the PNPs was measured. The decay of the PL intensity displayed a double exponential fitting curve (Fig. S3). The average fluorescence lifetime of PNPs was 3.01 ns [(2.20 ns (86.6%) and 8.11 ns (13.4%)], indicating the existence of two types of emission state in PNPs, which agreed well with the results of PL spectra. In addition, the fluorescence lifetime of the PNPs under different excitation wavelengths were investigated. Fig. 3b showed that the maximum emission wavelength remained constant (425 nm) when the excitation wavelength varied from 280 to 330 nm. Moreover, the emission peak was around 450 nm (in the range of 445–465 nm) when the excitation wavelength changed from 370 to 400 nm. Thus, we studied the fluorescence lifetime of the PNPs at two emission wavelengths (425 and 450 nm) under two groups of excitation wavelengths (305, 320, 335, 350 nm) and (375, 390, 405 nm), respectively. As shown in Table S1, τ 1, τ 2 and τ average (detected at 425 nm) all decreased as the excitation wavelength changed from 305 to 350 nm. When the excitation wavelength varied from 375 to 405 nm, τ 1 (detected at 450 nm) increased slightly, while τ 2 (detected at 450 nm) increased at first and then decreased. It may be attributed to their maximum emission wavelength since the maximum emission wavelength was close to 450 nm, when the excitation wavelength was 390 nm. However, the τ average (detected at 450 nm) increased as the excitation wavelength increased and the pre-exponential factor determined the average lifetime.

The surface functional groups of the PNPs were investigated by FTIR. As shown in Fig. 3c, the FTIR spectra of PNPs were similar to which of the native BSA except for the emergence of the shift of the Amide I and Amide II. The results were consistent with the UV–vis spectra that amide bonds still existed in the PNPs. Interestingly, the Amide I and Amide II of the PNPs were overlapped. In detail, the Amide I shifted to low wavenumbers and Amide II changed to high wavenumbers, which could be a result of the aggregation of the proteins [34]. This phenomenon was rare and the secondary structure of the PNPs may disappear because of the high reaction temperature. To verify this hypothesis, CD spectra were conducted to analyze the secondary structure of the PNPs. As shown in Fig. 3d, the absorption peak at 208, 222 nm disappeared compared with the native BSA, demonstrating the disappearance of the secondary structure of the PNPs [35]. Three aspects may account for this: first, the reaction temperature (80 °C) was higher than the temperature for conformation changes and aggregation of BSA [36]; second, the reaction time (60 h) was too long for BSA to keep native secondary structures [37]; third, the high concentration of NaOH (2 mol L−1) would disrupt the secondary structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism study on the origin of PL
ce:section.ce:para: To clarify the PL origin of the PNPs, control experiments were conducted. As we all know, native BSA consists of three kinds of aromatic amino acids (tryptophan, tyrosine and phenylalanine). And they were used as precursors to perform control experiments. The PL spectra of the crude product of PNPs were shown in Fig. 4 a. They presented excitation dependent PL properties as the excitation wavelength changed from 365 nm to 455 nm. Interestingly, when the mass of the three kinds of aromatic amino acids was equal to which of the BSA contained in the process of preparation of PNPs, the crude product exhibited excitation dependent PL properties and an emission peak at 490 nm (Fig. 4b), which was similar to which of the crude product of PNPs. However, the PL intensity of the crude product of PNPs was 10 times higher. Therefore, we speculated that the fluorescence of the supernatant of PNPs mainly came from the peptides of the three kinds of aromatic amino acids. Subsequently, we used the equal mass of tyrosine and phenylalanine to carry out the experiment. As displayed in Fig. 4c, the crude product showed excitation independent PL properties. Then, 10 times of tryptophan was applied to conduct the experiment and the crude product showed obvious excitation dependent PL properties (Fig. 4d). It is widely acknowledged that the fluorescence of BSA mainly comes from tryptophan, which could only emit as far as 365 nm [38,39]. Here, the prepared PNPs could emit visible fluorescence at 426 nm and it was a large red shift (61 nm). The emission wavelength could even shift to about 500 nm as the excitation wavelength changed to 455 nm. So the fluorescence origin of the PNPs was probably derived from the big conjugates containing the three kinds of aromatic amino acids.

It is necessary to study the stability of the PNPs before their practical use. Thus, the PL stability of the PNPs under various conditions was studied. As displayed in Fig. 5, the PL intensity of the PNPs kept constant in the pH range from 4 to 8. It is widely acknowledged most of nanoparticles enter the cells via endocytosis with the participation of lysosome [43]. The pH value in lysosome is about 4.5–5.0 and many fluorescent materials would lose their fluorescence at such pH [44]. The excellent PL stability of the PNPs made it possible to possess their fluorescence even in the lysosome. The effects of various external cations on the PL intensity of the PNPs were also studied. As shown in Fig. S6a, the PL intensity of the PNPs remained stable even the concentrations of cations rose to 30 μmol L−1. The PL intensity of the PNPs decreased slightly under continuous irradiation at 365 nm UV light for 3 h, which revealed the PNPs had good photostability (Fig. S6b). This property could ensure the stability of their fluorescent signal in the application process. There was almost no change in PL intensity of the PNPs at different concentration of KCl (aq) (Fig. S6c) and PBS (Fig. S12), demonstrating that the PNPs were stable under physiological conditions (Fig. S12). The fluorescence intensity of the CNP in PBS (Fig. S15) and different concentrations of KCl (aq) solution (Fig. S16) were also measured. The results indicated that the fluorescence intensities of the CNP almost remained stable under both conditions, displaying good stabilities. In addition, the PL property of PNPs was also kept in DMEM. These excellent tolerances to pH in a wide range, high ionic strength, external cations, UV irradiation, PBS buffer and DMEM culture made the PNPs exhibit great potential in biomedical use.

MTT experiments were conducted before the PNPs were introduced to biomedical application. As shown in Fig. S7, PNPs do not have obvious toxic effects to HeLa and GES-1 cells. Even the concentration went up to 200 μg/ml, the cell viability almost still remained 100%, indicating that the PNPs were nontoxic to the two kinds of cells. As displayed in Fig. 8 a and b, when the HeLa cells were incubated with PNPs for 4 h, no fluorescence was observed by confocal microscopy indicating no cellular uptake of PNPs. Even the incubation time was prolonged to 24 h, none fluorescence of PNPs was observed (results not shown). This may be because that the PNPs were prepared by using BSA as starting materials, and BSA could not enter into the cells. He and his colleagues also observed a similar phenomenon that BSA-FITC could not enter into the cells, which further support our result [45]. However, intense blue fluorescence of PNPs could be detected in cytoplasm of HeLa cells when Glucose Oxidase (GO) (0.2 ng.mL−1) were co-incubated with PNPs (Fig. 8c, d). Besides, the PL intensity increased as the concentration of GO increased along with the serious cell damage and death (results not shown). GO could catalytic oxidizing glucose to form gluconic acid and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) [46]. The culture media contained glucose, which was the substrate for GO. The byproduct of oxidation of glucose by GO in the media would be H2O2, which could induce transient permeabilization of cell membranes and facilitate the uptake of certain ligands into the cells that are otherwise not internalized [47,48]. Deshapriya and his colleagues observed the process that nBSA-FITC nanoparticles internalization (in the presence of nGO nanoparticles) by HN12 cells was accompanied with vesicular out-pocketing of the plasma membrane (blebbing) and they attributed the blebbing to the transient permeabilization of the plasma membrane [16]. Therefore, the uptake of PNPs might be induced by oxidative stress. So, this process may be applied to recognize cell oxidation damage through cell imaging. Subsequently, the uptake experiment of PNPs under the H2O2 stress was designed. The concentration of H2O2 and the incubation time (causing cellular oxidation damage) were according to our previous report [49]. Bright fluorescence of PNPs was also observed in cells (Fig. S8), which indicated that the PNPs might have a potential to recognize oxidation damage cells. To exclude the effects of the cell lines, similar experiments were conducted using GES-1 cells. As shown in Fig. S9, with the incubation of PNPs, no cellular uptake of PNPs was observed by confocal microscopy. But bright fluorescence of the PNPs in GES-1 cells was observed in the presence of GO or H2O2 (Fig. S10 and S11). However, the CNP could emit blue fluorescence when they entered cells, which couldn’t be applied for recognition of cellular oxidation damage (Fig. S17). In addition, both these two cell lines have been used for many imaging studies. Nagai and his colleagues have compared the uptake of carbon nanotubes by a wide range of cell lines and they concluded that the uptake depended on the charge and the size of the nanotubes, but not the cell types [50]. Thus, it was reasonable that HeLa and GES-1 cells behaved a similar manner toward PNPs.

In summary, blue fluorescent PNPs were synthesized through a one-pot method without introducing additional fluorescent molecules. The mechanism studies indicated that the PNPs contained two types of emissive centers and the origin of PL properties of the PNPs may probably come from the big conjugates containing the three kinds of aromatic amino acids. Besides, The PNPs exhibited excellent stability in severe conditions such as high ion strength, broad pH, various kinds of cations and UV irradiation. The PNPs were not toxic to the HeLa and GES-1 cell lines, presenting good biocompatibility. Furthermore, the PNPs could be applied to recognize the oxidation damage in cells, showing great potential use as clinical diagnostic agents. Now, we are trying to improve the properties of the PNPs for the purpose of using as a diagnostic or therapeutic agent in the future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: These studies were carried out with the support of the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21873075), National Special Subject of Infectious Diseases (2018ZX10301405), Guangxi Science and Technology Project (GuiKeAD17195081) and Bagui Scholar Program of Guangxi Province (2016). We gratefully acknowledge Dr. Cai-Fen Xia for her help of measuring the decay of PL intensity of PNPs.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2019.01.065.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: PEGylated WS2 nanosheets as a multifunctional theranostic agent for in vivo dual‐modal CT/photoacoustic imaging guided photothermal therapy
Combining magnetic resonance imaging with photothermal therapy of CuS@BSA nanoparticles for cancer theranostics
A chelator-free multifunctional [64Cu] CuS nanoparticle platform for simultaneous micro-PET/CT imaging and photothermal ablation therapy
PET/SPECT imaging agents for neurodegenerative diseases
Enhancing therapeutic efficacy of combined cancer phototherapy by ultrasound-mediated in situ conversion of near-infrared cyanine/porphyrin microbubbles into nanoparticles
Intrinsically radioactive [64Cu] CuInS/ZnS quantum dots for PET and optical imaging: improved radiochemical stability and controllable cerenkov luminescence
Rapid aqueous synthesis of CuInS/ZnS quantum dots as sensor probe for alkaline phosphatase detection and targeted imaging in cancer cells
Bovine serum albumin as a versatile platform for cancer imaging and therapy
Albumin nanoparticles targeted with apo E enter the CNS by transcytosis and are delivered to neurones
Active targeting schemes for nanoparticle systems in cancer therapeutics
Enzyme delivery using the 30Kc19 protein and human serum albumin nanoparticles
Molecular switch for the assembly of lipophilic drug incorporated plasma protein nanoparticles and in vivo image
Cationic bovine serum albumin based self-assembled nanoparticles as siRNA delivery vector for treating lung metastatic cancer
Vortex fluidic mediated synthesis of macroporous bovine serum albumin-based microspheres
